Research Statement

Research Statement

Research Motivation

My research is motivated by a central question: how can sound theory and rigorous empirical tools assist in designing systems conducive to human flourishing? Addressing the wicked problems of poverty, chronic disease, and extreme inequality requires, in addition to a heavy dose of empathy, an accurate model of reality. I am drawn to issues at the intersection of public health and anti-poverty policy, with a substantive focus on socioeconomic inequality, disability, and the institutional arrangements that shape life outcomes. Across projects, I aim to produce work that is empirically rigorous, transparent in its assumptions, reproducible, and directly relevant to decision-makers.

My training in public policy has pushed me toward an applied model of research, one that values strong research design and attends equally to the practical demands of producing evidence that can support program evaluation, public communication, and stakeholder use. As a result, my work sits at the intersection of analytic research and translational policy work. In practice, this has meant building reproducible data pipelines, conducting quantitative analyses, developing dashboards and accessible reports, and framing findings in ways that connect technical evidence to concrete public problems.

Disability Inequality & ADA-PARC

A central example of this approach is my work on the ADA-PARC project at the Nathalie P. Voorhees Center for Neighborhood and Community Improvement at the University of Illinois Chicago. ADA-PARC is a national research effort examining quality-of-life disparities between people with and without disabilities using 2023 American Community Survey data across national, regional, state, and city geographies. As the primary analyst and author, I built a large Census-based analytic pipeline, produced accessible reporting, and developed interactive tools including an R Shiny website and Tableau dashboards designed to translate complex disability-related disparities into formats usable by researchers, advocates, and policymakers alike. The project spans domains including community living, community participation, and work and economic outcomes, enabling a multidimensional view of inequality rather than a narrow focus on any single indicator.

This work has been formative in shaping how I think about policy research. Disability inequality is often discussed in fragmented terms, but the underlying patterns cut across employment, education, transportation, and community involvement in ways that reinforce one another. Working on ADA-PARC deepened my interest in research that can surface where disparities are most severe, how they cluster geographically and demographically, and what kinds of systems appear to shape them. It also reinforced a lesson that is sometimes overlooked in applied work: for findings to be maximally impactful, they must be communicated in a manner that is widely interpretable.

Public Health & Behavioral Risk

A second strand of my work focuses on public health outcomes and their social determinants. In an independent study project on the correlates of mood disorders, I used CDC health survey data to model the behavioral and demographic predictors of Generalized Anxiety Disorder and Major Depressive Disorder. The analysis distinguished modifiable risk factors including physical activity, sleep, and social connection from non-modifiable demographic characteristics, with the goal of identifying more actionable entry points for prevention-oriented policy.

What interested me most in this project was not the modeling exercise itself, but the underlying tension it surfaces: much of what drives mental health outcomes at the population level reflects social conditions and institutional arrangements, yet prevention policy is often designed with a focus on downstream individual behavior. That tension between structural context and individual risk is one I find consistently important across policy domains. Excellent policy design, particularly in the realm of public health, identifies the critical intervention point farthest up the causal chain. It asks not just how to handle poor outcomes, but how to prevent them from occurring in the first place.

Program Evaluation & Causal Inference

My interest in moving from description to evaluation is reflected in a program evaluation project on the Special Supplemental Nutrition Program for Women, Infants, and Children (WIC). Working with two collaborators, I applied a regression discontinuity design using synthetic data structured around WIC’s income-eligibility threshold to estimate potential effects on infant birth-weight outcomes. The project included a program logic model, a causal identification strategy, and robustness checks including bandwidth sensitivity and placebo tests. Using synthetic rather than administrative data was a deliberate methodological choice that allowed us to demonstrate the full evaluative framework under realistic structural constraints, a common challenge in applied policy research settings.

The project sharpened my understanding of how quasi-experimental methods can support credible inference when randomization is not available, a situation that characterizes most policy evaluation. More broadly, it reinforced my commitment to asking not only what is happening, but whether specific policies and institutional arrangements are actually reducing the inequities they are designed to address.

Looking Forward

Taken together, these projects reflect the kind of work I hope to continue building. I am most interested in research roles where I can contribute to rigorous applied analysis on questions of physical and mental health, inequality, and social policy, and where the expectation is not only to analyze data, but to help shape how evidence is interpreted and used. I am drawn to policy labs, think tanks, and applied research organizations because they offer a setting where methodological discipline and clear communication are both valued, and where findings are expected to reach audiences beyond academic journals.

Methodologically, I want to keep strengthening my skills in quantitative analysis, program evaluation, and reproducible research workflows, with particular attention to causal inference methods and spatial analysis of inequality. Substantively, I hope to build deeper expertise in how public systems shape economic security, health, and social inclusion.

In the longer term, I intend to pursue doctoral study in public policy, human development, or a closely related field. My aim is to develop a research program oriented around a central question: how do social conditions such as poverty, instability, and constrained opportunity shape health, psychological well-being, and long-term life trajectories, and to what extent can upstream policy interventions disrupt those pathways? I am drawn to work that bridges applied econometrics and the social determinants of health, using tools like quasi-experimental design and longitudinal data to evaluate how programs that strengthen the basic conditions of life including income, housing, and healthcare affect individual and communal well-being over time. Ultimately, I want to produce scholarship that makes inequality more legible, more solvable, and more central to public decision-making, grounded always in the conviction that the precursors to flourishing are owed to every human being.
